findings.headline: Toronto's heritage system isn't asymmetric. It's hierarchical.
findings.body: Moss Park has 209 City-designated heritage properties on its 379 parcels. Eglinton W / Little Jamaica: 0 on 189. Affluent residential gets conservation districts. Racialized commercial gets demolition permits.

findings.headline: The cultural corridor most exposed to development
findings.body: Spadina-Dundas / West Chinatown carries a Risk Index of 79 — among the highest displacement pressures in the city. $1476M in unused buildable envelope, 23 storefronts closed in the last 90 days, 14 heritage protections.

findings.headline: Where developer money is filing in 2026
findings.body: Weston: 29 new development applications filed in the last twelve months. The applicants are public record — names appear in the live ledger below.

findings.headline: 435 storefronts closed in 90 days
findings.body: College W / Little Italy: 59 cancellations in the last quarter — among the fastest erosion in the city. Named businesses and ages-at-closure in the live ledger.

pullQuotes.text: Critics slam 'ridiculous' decision to exclude Little Jamaica from mandatory affordable housing plan
The 'Likkle but Talawa' Community: Little Jamaica, Toronto's Black Cultural District, is on the Verge of Disappearing
pullQuotes.source: Society for the Study of Architecture in Canada (2024)

pullQuotes.text: The construction of the LRT has exposed anti-Black racism and heightened local socio-economic vulnerabilities, revealing the gentrifying effects of the Eglinton Crosstown's approach to transportation planning and urban policy.
pullQuotes.source: The Impact of Transit Development on Racialized Neighbourhoods (York University)

pullQuotes.text: How Little Jamaica is preserving local culture with Toronto's first Black-led land trust.
